coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical enantiorecognition of tryptophan enantiomers based on graphene quantum dots–chitosan composite film
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ou, Jie
Tao, Yongxin
Xue, Jingjing
Kong, Yong
Dai, Jiangying
Deng, Linhong
coredata.dc:description: A graphene quantum dots (GQDs)–chitosan (CS) composite film was prepared via successive electrodeposition of GQDs and CS on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The strong interactions between GQDs and CS resulted in the formation of a regular and uniform film, which can be applied in the electrochemical chiral recognition of tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers. CS in the composite film provides a chiral microenvironment, meanwhile, GQDs can amplify the electrochemical signals and improve the recognition efficiency. Due to the synergetic effect of GQDs and CS, chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers is achieved successfully. Compared with previous reports utilizing GQDs in photoluminescent research, this work opens a new avenue for broadening the applications of GQDs in the electrochemically chiral sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Chitosan
Chiral recognition
Tryptophan enantiomers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SEM and electrochemical characteristics of GQDs–CS/GCE
Electrochemical chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration for construction of chiral interface and recognition of Trp enantiomers. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of GQDs. (C) FT-IR spectra of GQDs and CA. (D) TEM image of GQDs, and the inset is the particle size histogram.

SEM images of CS (A), GQDs (B) and GQDs–CS (C) modified GCE. (D) Cyclic voltammograms of bare GCE (a), GQDs (b), GQDs–CS (c), and CS (d) modified GCE in 0.1M KCl containing 10mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. (E) Nyquist plots of bare GCE (a), GQDs (b), GQDs–CS (c), and CS (d) modified GCE in 0.1M KCl containing 10mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. Frequency range: 105 Hz–0.01Hz; applied potential: 0.2V.
Differential pulse voltammograms of l-Trp and d-Trp at bare GCE (A), GQDs (B), CS (C) and GQDs–CS (D) modified GCE.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical enantiorecognition of tryptophan enantiomers based on graphene quantum dots–chitosan composite film

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Changzhou Key Laboratory of Respiratory Medical Engineering, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Changzhou Key Laboratory of Respiratory Medical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A graphene quantum dots (GQDs)–chitosan (CS) composite film was prepared via successive electrodeposition of GQDs and CS on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The strong interactions between GQDs and CS resulted in the formation of a regular and uniform film, which can be applied in the electrochemical chiral recognition of tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers. CS in the composite film provides a chiral microenvironment, meanwhile, GQDs can amplify the electrochemical signals and improve the recognition efficiency. Due to the synergetic effect of GQDs and CS, chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers is achieved successfully. Compared with previous reports utilizing GQDs in photoluminescent research, this work opens a new avenue for broadening the applications of GQDs in the electrochemically chiral sensors.

Chitosan provides a chiral microenvironment, meanwhile, graphene quantum dots can amplify the electrochemical signals. Due to the synergetic effect of chitosan and graphene quantum dots, chiral recognition of tryptophan enantiomers is achieved successfully.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a class of “zero-dimensional” carbon material which have recently attracted increasing attention due to their outstanding properties, such as excellent photostability and water solubility, biocompatibility and low toxicity [1,2]. Recent research on GQDs is mainly focused on photoluminescence because of the fluorescent characteristic of GQDs [3–5]. Though GQDs have been applied in electrochemical sensing [6–8], the applications of GQDs in electrochemical enantiorecognition have not been explored until very recently, which might be due to the absence of chiral center in GQDs. Recently, Trojanowicz [9] reviewed that successful chiral recognition can be achieved via combining multi-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and optically active substances, opening a new avenue in electrochemical enantiorecognition with modified GQDs.

As a kind of biocompatible polysaccharide, chitosan (CS) exhibits excellent chiral selectivity attributed to the existence of a large quantity of chiral sites [10], and it has been widely adopted as chiral stationary phase for chromatographic techniques [11–14]. As far as we know, little or no attention has been paid to the use of CS for electrochemical recognition of chiral compounds due to the dielectric and insulating features of CS. However, a recently proposed strategy by our group using β-cyclodextrin, another kind of natural polysaccharide, combined with poly(l-glutamic acid) for the electrochemical enantiorecognition of tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers [15] suggests the feasibility of chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers with CS from the electrochemical point of view.

Herein, we first report on a chiral interface based on GQDs–CS composite film modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE), GQDs–CS/GCE, for recognizing Trp enantiomers. The electrodeposited GQDs–CS composite film combines the advantages of GQDs and CS, i.e., it not only possesses chiral center for recognition, but also has excellent electrical conductivity for amplifying electrochemical signals. Therefore, the composite film is applied for electrochemical enantiorecognition of Trp enantiomers, and the enantiorecognition ability of the GQDs–CS/GCE is evaluated by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), revealing a higher affinity to l-Trp.

All reagents used were analytical grade and used as received. Electrochemical experiments were performed with a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation in a three-electrode cell comprising of a GCE or modified GCE as working, a platinum foil as auxiliary and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference electrode, respectively. The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were recorded on a UV-2450 spectrometer and a F-280A fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. The FT-IR spectra were measured by a FTIR-8400S spectrometer. SEM and TEM images were recorded on a Supra55 field-emission scanning electron microscope and a JEM-2000 transmission electron microscope.

GQDs were prepared according to a bottom-up method previously reported by directly pyrolyzing citric acid (CA) [16]. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was carried out for the electrodeposition of GQDs on GCE (3mm diameter) from GQD aqueous solution (2gL−1) in the potential range from 0 to 1.0V at a scan rate of 100mVs−1. After 50 repeating cycles, the three-electrode system was transferred into 0.1M HCl containing 2gL−1 CS, and a negative potential of −0.5V was applied on the working electrode (GQDs/GCE) for 200s for the deposition of CS film and the construction of GQDs–CS/GCE.

The obtained GQDs–CS/GCE was immersed into a 20mL 0.1M phosphate buffer saline (PBS) containing 0.5mM l- or d-Trp (pH7.0) for 60s at 15°C, and then the electrochemical responses of l- and d-Trp combined with the GQDs–CS/GCE were monitored by DPV in the potential range from 0.4 to 1.0V with a step potential of 4mV and an amplitude of 50mV. The electrochemical enantiorecognition of Trp enantiomers is based on the differences of the oxidation peak currents of l- and d-Trp combined with the GQDs–CS/GCE. The stepwise procedure for construction of the chiral interface and recognizing Trp enantiomers is illustrated in Fig. 1A.

The typical UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of GQDs are shown in Fig. 1B. The GQDs exhibit a broad UV–vis absorption below 400nm without any obvious peak, implying that the sp2 clusters contained in GQDs are not uniform in size [16], and a weak shoulder at 344nm is attributed to the n–π* transition of the C=O band and the π–π* transition of the conjugated C=C band [17]. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths appear at 381 and 458nm, respectively. FT-IR spectra of GQDs and CA were recorded to obtain further structural insights of the prepared GQDs (Fig. 1C). A broad peak at 3341cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH from GQDs, and the two peaks at 1766 and 1716cm−1 are assigned to the antisymmetric stretching vibration and symmetric stretching vibration of C=O [18]. A small peak at 1631cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of C=C [17]. Additionally, the C–OH stretching vibration (1112cm−1) in CA is changed to the C–O–C stretching vibration (1182cm−1) in the GQDs, suggesting that CA is completely carbonized to GQDs after pyrolyzation treatment. TEM image (Fig. 1D) reveals that the GQDs have good monodispersity, and the average size of the dot-like GQDs is ~2.6nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM and electrochemical characteristics of GQDs–CS/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: SEM images of CS, GQDs and GQDs–CS modified GCE are shown in Fig. 2 . CS electrodeposited directly on GCE in the absence of GQDs as matrix displays a loose and irregular structure (Fig. 2A). If the GCE is coated with GQDs beforehand, a compact film of GQDs can be observed (Fig. 2B), and the following deposition of CS on the GQDs modified GCE results in the formation of an interwoven net-like structure of GQDs–CS with regularly embedded cavities (Fig. 2C), providing an efficient interface for the following combination with Trp enantiomers. Obviously, GQDs play a key role in the formation of regular and high ordered GQDs–CS composite film. On the one hand, oxygen-containing groups on GQDs can provide large amounts of active sites for CS deposition; on the other hand, the electron-withdrawing oxygen-containing groups on GQDs are also beneficial to the combination with CS from the electrostatic point of view, since CS dissolved in acid (HCl in the present work) is positively charged [19].

[Fe(CN)6]3−/4− is a commonly used redox couple to investigate the electrochemical performances of modified electrodes. Herein, the cyclic voltammograms of bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, CS/GCE and GQDs–CS/GCE were measured in 0.1M KCl containing 10mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (Fig. 2D). A pair of well-defined redox peaks appears at the bare GCE with a peak potential separation (ΔE p) of 102mV (curve a). After GQD modification, the peak currents decrease significantly accompanied by a remarkable increase in ΔE p to 383mV due to the repulsion between the oxygen-containing groups on GQDs and the anions of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (curve b), which is also consistent with previous report [20]. After successive modification of GQDs and CS, a partial restoration of peak currents and ΔE p (153mV) is observed (curve c), which is owing to the affinity between positively charged CS and negatively charged [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. However, compared with bare GCE, the peak currents at the GQDs–CS/GCE still decrease a little, providing an indirect proof that the firstly deposited GQDs still attach to the GCE surface after the following modification of CS. When CS is modified to GCE, maximum peak currents and minimum ΔE p (96mV) are observed (curve d), which can be ascribed to the strong electrostatic interactions between positively charged CS and [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is used for further characterization of each step during GCE modification (Fig. 2E). For bare and modified GCE, the charge transfer resistance Rct, an indicator reflecting the resistance at the electrode–solution interface, is arranged in such an order: GQDs/GCE (5207Ω)>GQDs–CS/GCE (274Ω)>bare GCE (78Ω)>CS/GCE (51Ω). The results of EIS agree well with those of cyclic voltammograms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical responses of l- or d-Trp combined with the GQDs–CS/GCE were recorded by DPV at 15°C. For comparison of the recognition efficiency, several other electrodes including bare GCE, GQDs/GCE and CS/GCE were used for the control experiments. For bare GCE, it is found that the two oxidation peaks of l- and d-Trp are completely overlapped (Fig. 3A). Although the peak currents are remarkably increased at the GQDs/GCE due to the excellent electrical conductivity of GQDs, the ΔE p between l- and d-Trp is still too small (4mV) to distinguish the enantiomers effectively (Fig. 3B). From the above two figures, it can be concluded that without optically active substance, bare GCE or GQDs/GCE cannot result in the successful enantiorecognition. When CS containing chiral center is used to modify GCE, the differences in both peak currents and ΔE p are obviously enlarged (Fig. 3C), suggesting that CS can provide a chiral microenvironment for stereoselective combination with Trp enantiomer. Owing to different steric hindrance during the combination with l- and d-Trp, the CS/GCE preferentially combines with l-Trp and results in larger peak current and lower peak potential of l-Trp compared with d-Trp. The recognition efficiency is further improved at the GQDs–CS/GCE with a peak current ratio (IL/ID) of 2.06 and ΔE p of 80mV (Fig. 3D), it is no doubt that both CS and GQDs play important roles in the improvement of chiral recognition efficiency. As a decisive chiral component in the composite film, CS provides a chiral platform for selective adsorption of Trp enantiomers; meanwhile, GQDs can amplify the electrochemical signals produced during the chiral sensing process. In one word, the synergetic effect of GQDs and CS leads to a successful and effective chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers.

In summary, a novel electrochemical chiral interface for effective recognition of Trp enantiomers is proposed in this work. The chiral interface is composed of GQDs and CS, which are successively electrodeposited on the surface of GCE. The GQDs–CS composite film combines the advantages of GQDs (excellent electrical conductivity) and CS (chiral center and chiral microenvironment), and thus it is applied successfully for the enantioselective recognition of Trp enantiomers. Since most research on GQDs is focused on photoluminescence because of the fluorescent characteristic of GQDs, this work may open new opportunities for broadening the applications of GQDs to the field of electrochemical chiral sensors and bioelectrochemistry. Meanwhile, this work provides beneficial reference for chiral sensing of other chiral compounds in chiral assays, pharmaceutical assays and clinical industry.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21275023), Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology (BM2012110), Changzhou Key Laboratory of Respiratory Medical Engineering (CM20133005) and Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).
